content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 9, 1992

Honorable Richard G. Polanco
Assemblymember, 55th District
State Capitol
P.O. Box 942849
Sacramento, CA  94249-0001

                                          Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                              Our File No. I-92-459

Dear Assemblymember Polanco:

     You have requested additional guidance with respect to the
responsibilities of members of the Latino Legislative Caucus,
concerning the gift and honoraria limits of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹  This letter is intended to supplement the
advice provided in the Polanco Advice Letter, No. A-92-433.  Since
you have requested this clarification on behalf of unnamed
candidates, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.²

                                  QUESTION

     What are the responsibilities of local elected officers and
candidates for elected office who receive gifts of travel from the
Mexican Government in connection with a meeting on the North
American Free Trade Agreement?


_____________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
"immunity" provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95812  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-92-459
Page 2


CONCLUSION

Conflict of Interest Requirements: Both the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act apply to all public
officials, including local elected officers. Therefore, a local
elected officer who receives a gift of free transportation will be
required to disclose the gift and disqualify himself or herself
from any decision which would have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on the donor.

Generally, candidates for elective office will be subject to
the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act once
they assume office.³ At that time, the candidate will have an
economic interest in any donor of gifts and sources of income over
the past 12 months. This interest must be disclosed on the public
official's assuming office statement and may result in a conflict
of interest.

Gift Limits: Section 89501 provides that local elected officers
may not receive gifts and honoraria aggregating more than $1,000
from any single source in a calendar year. However, Section 89501
exempts gifts of actual travel expenses and reasonable subsistence
in connection with attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering, from
the gift limits.

Moreover, the gift limits of the Act only apply to local
elected officers, elected state officers, designated employees of
state agencies, and members of state boards of commissions. Thus,
a candidate who does not currently hold any of those positions is
not subject to the gift limits of the Act.

FACTS

In our August 12, 1992, letter to you (Polanco Advice Letter,
supra) we advised you with respect to the responsibilities of
legislators regarding the receipt of a payment for travel and
accommodations made by the Mexican Government. The travel was in
connection with a meeting on the North American Free Trade
Agreement.

In our telephone conversation of August 28, 1992, you asked
for clarification of the advice with respect to attendees who were
not legislators, but were local elected officers and candidates
for an elective office.


³ Section 87201 provides that candidates for certain elective
offices are required to disclose investments and interests in real
property even prior to being elected to office. (See also,
Section 87200.)

Our File No. I-92-459
Page 3


ANALYSIS

Conflict-of-Interest Provisions

     The disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act
apply to all public officials, including local elected officers.
(Section 87100; Section 87200; Section 87300.) Therefore, a local
elected officer who receives free transportation will be required
to disclose the gift (Section 87207) and to disqualify himself or
herself from any decision which would have a foreseeable and
material financial effect on the donor during the 12 months
following the receipt of the gift. (See, Polanco Advice Letter,
supra.)

     Additionally, Section 87201 provides that candidates for
certain elective offices are required to disclose investments and
interests in real property even prior to being elected to office.4
(See also, Section 87200.) However, most other candidates will
not be required to file disclosure statements until they are
elected to office.

     In any case, once a candidate assumes office, the candidate
will be required to disclose any donor of gifts and sources of
income over the past 12 months. Thus, the gift from the Mexican
Government would be disclosed at the time the candidate assumes
office.

     In addition, the candidate will be subject to the
disqualification provisions of the Act once the candidate takes
office. Thus, the receipt of a gift may result in
disqualification for 12 months after receipt of the gift.
(Section 87100; Section 87103.)

Gift Limits

     Section 89501 provides:

          No local elected officeholder shall accept any
     honoraria for any speech given, article published,
     or attendance at any public or private conference,
     convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
     gathering, or any gifts, from any single source,
     which is in excess of one thousand dollars


4    Assembly Bill 1075 (Clute) would also require these candidates
     to disclose sources of any income received during the 12 months
     immediately preceding the filing of the candidate statement of
     economic interests. AB 1075 was passed by the Legislature and has
     been enrolled.

Our File No. I-92-459
Page 4


($1,000), in any calendar year, except
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.

According to Section 89501, local elected officers may not
receive honoraria and gifts aggregating more than $1,000 from any
single source in a calendar year. However, Section 89501 exempts
gifts of actual travel expenses and reasonable subsistence in
connection with attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering, from
the gift limits of Section 89501.5  (Regulation 18950.2.)

Moreover, the gift limits of the Act only apply to local
elected officers, elected state officers, designated employees of
state agencies, and members of state boards of commissions.  Thus,
a person who does not hold one of those positions is not subject
to the gift limits of the Act.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.6

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By:  John W. Wallace
     Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa


5     Please note that the express language of the exceptions set
forth in Section 89506 is limited to elected state officers,
members of a state boards and commissions, and designated
employees of a state agencies.  (Section 89506(a)(1).)

     Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
